data.content: In 1H25, global maritime oil trade reached 79.8 million barrels per day (b/d), accounting for 76% of total world oil supply (104.4 million b/d). The Strait of Malacca remained the world’s busiest chokepoint, handling 23.2 million b/d—29% of seaborne oil—primarily carrying Middle Eastern crude to China (48% of imports), India, and East Asia. LNG flows through Malacca totaled 9.2 Bcf/d, with Qatar-to-China shipments doubling since 2020. The Strait of Hormuz, critical for Persian Gulf exports, averaged 20.9 million b/d in 1H25 (20% of global consumption), with 89% bound for Asia (China, India, Japan, South Korea). Crude flows declined post-2022 due to OPEC+ cuts and regional pipeline alternatives (e.g., Saudi East-West pipeline: 4.7 million b/d capacity). Iran’s exports via Hormuz rose despite sanctions, while U.S. imports from the Gulf fell to 40-year lows. Red Sea disruptions since late 2023 sharply reduced flows through the Suez Canal/SUMED (4.9 million b/d in 1H25) and Bab el-Mandeb (4.2 million b/d)—both down ~50% from 2023—as ships rerouted around the Cape of Good Hope, which saw flows surge to 9.1 million b/d in 1H25. Russian crude exports via Bab el-Mandeb and Suez remained stable due to limited Houthi targeting. The Danish Straits handled 4.9 million b/d in 1H25, up 60% since 2021, as Russia redirected oil from Europe to Asia and India, while U.S. LNG exports to Northern Europe tripled to 1.6 Bcf/d. Turkish Straits saw 3.7 million b/d, driven by rising exports from Kazakhstan and Russia via Black Sea ports. Panama Canal flows stabilized at 2.3 million b/d, though droughts increased shipping costs. Global oil transit remains vulnerable to geopolitical disruptions, with chokepoints like Hormuz and Malacca having no viable alternatives, while Cape of Good Hope serves as a costly but critical detour.

page_links.text: Amid regional conflict, the Strait of Hormuz remains critical oil chokepoint
About one-fifth of global liquefied natural gas trade flows through the Strait of Hormuz
Red Sea disruptions increase oil flows around Cape of Good Hope
Drought at the Panama Canal delays energy shipments, increasing shipping costs
U.S. energy flows through Panama Canal rose slightly in January
Red Sea attacks increase shipping times and freight rates
Longer maritime routes mean more fuel consumed in shipping following Red Sea attacks
